headline: Chips Swim Against the Tide as Most of the Market Slips
body_html:
Monday's midday session is shaping up as a broadly defensive day, with decliners outpacing advancers 295 to 205 across a 501-name universe. Seven of the eight tracked sectors are trading in the red, and Homebuilders are bearing the heaviest damage, off 1.72% intraday, with Materials and Industrials also nursing meaningful losses. The backdrop is a Middle East conflict that shows no sign of abating: Houthi forces have announced a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia and attacked tanker vessels off Oman, pushing U.S. pump prices back above four dollars per gallon for the first time in months.

The singular bright spot is Semiconductors, up 1.74% and pulling clear of every other sector. Credo Technology leads the entire leaderboard with a gain approaching 7%, flanked by MaxLinear, Fabrinet, Teradyne, and Marvell Technology, all up between roughly 5% and 6%. The cluster suggests the move is thematic — data-infrastructure and connectivity names catching a bid — rather than a broad risk-on rally.

On the downside, solar names are taking the sharpest punishment: SolarEdge has shed more than 9% and Enphase Energy over 5%, dragging clean-energy sentiment lower. Treasury yields are edging higher as investors price in sustained geopolitical risk. Into the close, watch whether the semiconductor bid holds and whether any ceasefire-mediation headlines out of the Middle East shift the energy and broader tape.

briefs.headline: Houthi Blockade Opens Dangerous New Front in Gulf Shipping
briefs.body: Yemen's Houthi forces declared a naval blockade against Saudi Arabia on Monday and claimed attacks on two tanker vessels off Oman, escalating the maritime dimension of the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict. The moves rattled energy-shipping routes and contributed to a renewed climb in domestic gasoline prices, which crossed the four-dollar-per-gallon threshold this session.

briefs.headline: Solar Sector Hammered; SolarEdge Leads Decliners Down Nearly 10%
briefs.body: SolarEdge is the session's worst performer, tumbling 9.16% intraday, with Enphase Energy close behind at minus 5.52%. The synchronized selloff points to sector-specific pressure rather than a single catalyst, continuing a difficult stretch for residential and commercial solar names amid rising rates and policy uncertainty.

briefs.headline: Mediators Float 10-Day Ceasefire to Salvage Iran-U.S. Interim Agreement
briefs.body: A senior Iranian official told Reuters that mediators have proposed a 10-day pause in hostilities designed to restart negotiations over an interim deal between Tehran and Washington. Markets have so far responded cautiously; Treasury yields continued to drift higher even as the headline briefly circulated, suggesting traders want tangible progress before repositioning.

briefs.headline: Credo Technology Surges Nearly 7%, Anchors Semiconductor Leadership
briefs.body: Credo Technology's nearly 7% intraday advance is the standout single-stock move of the morning, helping lift the broader Semiconductors sector to a gain of 1.74% — the only sector in positive territory. Connectivity and optical-networking peers MaxLinear, Fabrinet, and Marvell Technology are all posting gains above 5%, reinforcing the data-infrastructure theme.

briefs.headline: Spain Claims World Cup Title Over Argentina
briefs.body: Spain defeated Argentina in Sunday's World Cup final, capturing the tournament trophy in a result that captured global attention heading into the Monday session. The outcome had no direct market impact but dominated social and broadcast media, a backdrop noted by strategists tracking consumer-sentiment indicators tied to major sporting events.

briefs.headline: UK Faces Leadership Change Amid Sharp U.S. Criticism
briefs.body: Britain is set to install a new prime minister after a political transition that drew pointed commentary from U.S. President Donald Trump, who described the country in notably harsh terms. Investors are watching the transition for any implications for the U.S.-UK trade relationship and broader sterling-denominated assets, though the pound's reaction has been measured so far.
